title: Head Path Template Showcase
description: Realistic structured-template review set for the head path: philosophy, models, inquiry, epistemology, and conceptual clarity.

slides.title: Testing A View Of Consciousness
slides.subtitle: An inquiry opener for models, assumptions, comparisons, and conceptual clarity.
slides.body: We begin with the question, name the candidate views, test what each view must explain, and keep the central phenomenon visible while the argument develops.

body.text: A good theory should make experience more intelligible without pretending the mystery has disappeared.
The head path needs precision, but precision should keep inquiry open rather than brittle.
Any adequate view of consciousness has to account for first-person experience, public evidence, causal regularities, and the fact that models are themselves appearances within experience.
A strong head slide should make the structure of the question visible before arguing for an answer.
The goal is not to win a metaphysical debate on one slide, but to clarify what each view must explain.
Do not start with the preferred answer.
Start with the phenomenon that would remain even if the answer changed.

slides.title: The World As It Appears
slides.body: Full-image slides can make a conceptual question feel vast before narrowing into distinctions.

slides.title: Experience And Explanation
slides.body: Split-image is useful when a model needs both a visual anchor and a concise set of distinctions.
bullets: Name the cost of the explanation.

body.text: Every model of the world also implies a model of the knower.
This panel should make philosophical content feel as visually strong as the older image-led slides while staying clear and restrained.

slides.title: Four Questions For Any View
slides.body: Title-bullets should make conceptual evaluation feel orderly and memorable.
bullets: What does the view say exists?
How does it treat first-person evidence?
Which anomaly is hardest for it?

slides.title: Distinctions To Keep Apart
slides.body: Use the basic bullet template when the content is a list of discriminations.

slides.title: View Comparison Notes
slides.body: Dense-bullets should let a complex discussion remain scannable.

slides.title: Public Evidence And Private Experience
slides.subtitle: The question lives between method and immediacy.

columns.title: Public Evidence
columns.body: Repeatable, shareable, measurable patterns that make science possible.

columns.title: Private Experience
columns.body: The felt presence of seeing, hearing, thinking, pain, meaning, and selfhood.

columns.title: Live Problem
columns.body: A theory must respect both without quietly deleting either side.

slides.title: Testing A Metaphysical Claim
slides.body: Steps can turn philosophical analysis into a repeatable practice.

steps.title: State
steps.text: Put the claim in one sentence.

steps.title: Define
steps.text: Clarify the key terms before evaluating it.

steps.title: Derive
steps.text: Name what should follow if it is true.

steps.title: Compare
steps.text: Set it beside at least one rival view.

steps.title: Revise
steps.text: Adjust the claim or its scope.

slides.title: Conceptual Quality Check
slides.body: Checklist slides should make standards explicit before the debate begins.

items.title: Scope
items.text: The claim says what it does not cover.

items.title: Rivals
items.text: At least one plausible alternative is named.

items.title: Evidence
items.text: The argument distinguishes data from interpretation.

slides.title: Is The View Doing Enough Work?

stats.label: hard anomalies
stats.note: where the view pays its cost

slides.title: Analytic Idealism
slides.body: A profile slide can introduce a view, thinker, or concept without turning it into an essay.
bullets: Strength: starts from what is given.

slides.title: Argument In Motion
slides.body: Media slides should give enough conceptual context for the clip to be worth watching.

slides.title: How A View Evolves
slides.body: Timeline works well for intellectual history, paradigm shifts, or argument flow.

timeline.title: Problem
timeline.body: A phenomenon resists the current model.

timeline.title: Distinction
timeline.body: A new conceptual separation becomes useful.

timeline.title: Model
timeline.body: The view organizes more of the evidence.

timeline.title: Anomaly
timeline.body: A hard case exposes the cost.

timeline.title: Revision
timeline.body: The view becomes sharper or gives way.

slides.title: Ontology Cross-Check
rows: Why experience is present at all
Why the world is stable and shared

slides.title: What Makes A View Better?
slides.body: Card grids help abstract criteria become concrete and comparable.

cards.title: Coherence
cards.body: The view does not contradict its own terms.

cards.title: Coverage
cards.body: It accounts for the relevant phenomena.

cards.title: Parsimony
cards.body: It adds no more machinery than needed.

cards.title: Fruitfulness
cards.body: It opens useful questions, practices, or predictions.

slides.title: Let The View Risk Something
slides.body: Choose the assumption your preferred view relies on most, then ask what you would notice differently if it were false.
slides.note: Good philosophy changes how the next observation is made.
